Lambda

Author

Leon Benito

Published

February 16, 2026

1 Introduction

In 2017, BoE Governor Mark Carney gave a speech titled ‘Lambda’ describing how BoE policy had been confronted with a trade-off between returning inflation to the inflation target and stabilising output and how the Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) confronted that trade-off.

In this note, I replicate and update parts of Governor Carney’s analysis. The period since Governor Carney’s speech has been marked by further episodes that imply a macro trade-off between reaching the inflation target and output stabilisation.

2 The macro-data: BoE forecasts of Inflation and Output gaps

Every three months since being made independent of the Government in 1997, the Bank of England has published forecasts for how it expects the UK economy to evolve. These forecasts are published as part of the Bank’s Monetary Policy Report (previously, Inflation Report). There has been only one exception since 1997 – in May 2020, at the height of the Covid-19 pandemic.

The plots below show the succession of these BoE forecasts – for CPI inflation, the level of GDP and the unemployment rate. The charts can be used to constructive a narrative of the various factors that shaped UK economic performance through this period, as well as the gradual process of learning their impact – as perceived by the Bank of England.

Reading 42 sheets

In order to focus on the UK’s more recent experience, it is helpful to ‘zoom-in’ on the most recent macro forecasts, as shown below.

How are output gaps and inflation related to one another? The charts below give an indication, distinguishing three different phases:
- pre-Global Financial Crisis (GFC), when inflation was close to target and output varied little around its potential level so the output gap was small;
- Post-GFC and pre-Covid (2008-19), when inflation was close to target despite the estimated output gap varying significantly;
- Covid-19 and post-pandemic when inflation rose significantly above target, with significant variation in the output gap.

The contrasting post-GFC period and the post-pandemic period points to negative supply shocks dominating the post-GFC period, while demand shocks dominated during the pandemic period.

The Lambda of Governor Carney’s speech refers to the relative weight the policymaker places on output stabilisation, relative to inflation stabilisation. So this captures the central bank’s preferences, in th eface of certain macro shocks.

# Introduction

In 2017, BoE Governor Mark Carney gave a [speech titled 'Lambda'](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/speech/2017/lambda) describing how BoE policy had been confronted with a trade-off between returning inflation to the inflation target and stabilising output and how the Bank's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) confronted that trade-off.

In this note, I replicate and update parts of Governor Carney's analysis. The period since Governor Carney's speech has been marked by further episodes that imply a macro trade-off between reaching the inflation target and output stabilisation.


# The macro-data: BoE forecasts of Inflation and Output gaps

Every three months since being made independent of the Government in 1997, the [Bank of England](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/) has published forecasts for how it expects the UK economy to evolve. These forecasts are published as part of the Bank's _Monetary Policy Report_ (previously, _Inflation Report_). There has been only one exception since 1997 -- in May 2020, at the height of the Covid-19 pandemic.

The plots below show the succession of these BoE forecasts -- for CPI inflation, the level of GDP and the unemployment rate. The charts can be used to constructive a narrative of the various factors that shaped UK economic performance through this period, as well as the gradual process of learning their impact -- as perceived by the Bank of England.



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Reading 42 sheets 
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Lambda_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png){width=864}
:::
:::


In order to focus on the UK's more recent experience, it is helpful to 'zoom-in' on the most recent macro forecasts, as shown below.



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Lambda_files/figure-html/zoom-1.png){width=864}
:::
:::


How are output gaps and inflation related to one another? The charts below give an indication, distinguishing three different phases:   
- **pre-Global Financial Crisis (GFC)**, when inflation was close to target and output varied little around its potential level so the output gap was small;   
- **Post-GFC and pre-Covid (2008-19)**, when inflation was close to target despite the estimated output gap varying significantly;   
- **Covid-19 and post-pandemic** when inflation rose significantly above target, with significant variation in the output gap.   

The contrasting post-GFC period and the post-pandemic period points to negative supply shocks dominating the post-GFC period, while demand shocks dominated during the pandemic period.   



::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Lambda_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=864}
:::
:::


The Lambda of Governor Carney's speech refers to the relative weight the
policymaker places on output stabilisation, relative to inflation stabilisation. So this captures the central bank's preferences, in th eface of certain macro shocks.


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Lambda_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=864}
:::
:::
